speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape two of an interview with Mrs. Rose Patoka on March 18, 1997. Mrs. Patoka, we had been talking about your time in Russia.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you mentioned that you and your younger brother-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] or was Lev your-- Lev your older brother or younger brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Younger brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Younger brother-- were working on farms.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you actually in Russia, because you left in June of '41, you said, from Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, and I came back in-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I came back in March-- March or April, '45.

speaker: interviewee
text: The war finished in-- in May, right?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: I came.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you came back right before that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right before that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in-- so you were in Russia almost four years.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, almost.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were the two of you together the whole time you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. He was there with me-- ah, did I tell you [NON-ENGLISH], where we'd been?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, you said you had been there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, he's been with us. Then we went to-- the Germans supposed to come there now. So we run away to Stalingrad, Volgograd. And over there, we'd been for a little time. And I saw [INAUDIBLE] again. And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] wait a minute. And then they called him to go in a-- in the army. But it wasn't an army like [INAUDIBLE]. There was a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [NON-ENGLISH]. There was like a-- a working place, like a working army, something.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm. So he left?

speaker: interviewee
text: And they took-- yes, they took him away. And then I was alone.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know approximately what year that was, that he-- you two were separated?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had been together.

speaker: interviewee
text: Later I had letters. We were together then. Then I had letters from him, that they took him in the army. And he was in Nikolaev. Nikolaev is near the Romanian border. It is the same place where our brother Avrum used to be. That's where he left. That's the last letter I had from him. I never found out where he went, where he'd gone.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you got that letter, were you-- was that a letter sent to you while you were in Russia?

speaker: interviewee
text: While I was still in Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you last heard from him in '44 or '45?

speaker: interviewee
text: Somewhere like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And to this day you've never--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Don't know what happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was looking for him everywhere, writing everywhere. The Russian commissary [INAUDIBLE] and there. [INAUDIBLE]. Must have gone in-- in the fight or something.

speaker: interviewer
text: After he left, who were you with? Were you with girlfriends, or relatives, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [INAUDIBLE]. I got the friended with some girlfriends from here, from there. One of them was from [INAUDIBLE], even. And then we decided to go back. But now, [NON-ENGLISH] wasn't free yet. The Germans were still there, in [NON-ENGLISH] and Holoby.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went with them to Odessa. There was free already. And then I went to Odessa, and I stayed there a little while. Then I started writing to Holoby [INAUDIBLE] and then-- and that's the way I got home. When you were in Russia, were you able to find out news of what was happening in Poland and other parts of Europe, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Very little, very little. I remember, to the end, I was reading about how the Jews put people in a cars-- I mean the Germans put people in cars and gassed them in cars. But I never knew about-- about the concentration camps or anything. I didn't hear that. I mean, I read in the paper about it. That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you came back from Russia--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said that was either March or April of 1945?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how is it that you made the decision to leave Russia and come back? Had the Germans left Holoby at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I was writing-- I was writing letters to Holoby.

speaker: interviewer
text: To your brother, or-- because you didn't know, at that point, what had happened to anyone.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I was writing to the government of Holoby. I don't know how you call it in here-- the government of Holoby. Then one girlfriend answered me. She's living, now, in Israel-- the one I went to school with. One girlfriend answered, and a Ukrainian boy answered the letter. They worked in the administration there. And they answered the letter, and they said that my family is gone. One of them is in Russia. One of them's there. But they knew about some people. Most of them, the Germans killed. They-- they were writing out everything in their letter, that-- who got killed, who living, and all that. And that's how I-- I found out about it and decided to go home, because the Germans weren't there anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you came home, where was that to?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't come home to the home, Holoby. I went to Kivertsi.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who was there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where my brother lived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which-- which brother was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: The brother-- the oldest one, Moshe. The one that-- he was a big shot on the railroad. So he came first. So he-- that was his wife's house in Kivertsi, a different city. That's where I went.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, had he been in a camp during the war, or he had been--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. He's been in Russia. He's been in--

speaker: interviewer
text: He was in Russia the whole time.

speaker: interviewee
text: The whole time.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you never saw him while you were in Russia.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you came, and you went to live with your brother and his family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and-- now then, who was living from your family, besides Moshe? You didn't know where Lev was.

speaker: interviewee
text: Lev? No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, you didn't know where he was.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: So besides you and Moshe, you said you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Nachman and Avrum.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, was Nachman-- he had been taken into the Russian army also.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, he was in the Polish army. From the Polish army, he went to Israel, through Iran, through Iran. At that time, they call it Persia or something. Through Iran, he went to-- to Israel, from the Polish army.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Nachman--

speaker: interviewee
text: Later I found out about him. After the war, I found out about him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you all together again in Poland, though?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Nachman didn't come back to Moshe's house. He was not there.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was you and Moshe. And where was Avrum?

speaker: interviewee
text: And Avrum was in jail. He was in the Russian jail, because somebody said that he's from this-- from the Ukraine where all them-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] all the Ukrainian people there they wanted an independent Ukraine, that he's from there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he's no good. He's not a good element for the Russian army. So they put him in jail, and that's where he was. Or something he said-- he said to a friend that he were-- they were looking at airplanes one day.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said, we got-- wait a minute. Oh, he said, our airplanes are not that good, as this one. [LAUGHS] And that's what I said. And they put him in the jail for that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you see him? Do you remember when you first saw him after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then later, it was amnesty in Russia, and they let him go. 1948 [INAUDIBLE] something. 1948, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your--

speaker: interviewee
text: And they let him go to Poland, back. He was in Lodz in Poland. That's when I started writing letters with him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your father, and your sister, and your brother had been--

speaker: interviewee
text: Killed, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Killed by the Germans.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Nazis killed them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, were they killed-- were they sent to-- to a camp, a death camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. They killed them in 1944, I think, in October. That day that he gave you, my-- [INAUDIBLE] gave you-- that date when all the Jews were killed in Holoby.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they were shot?

speaker: interviewee
text: And they were shot, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you know that that was what had happened to your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I didn't know at first. But later I found out. Later, when they wrote me letters, this girlfriend and this boyfriend, whatever, I had from school-- when they wrote me letters, they wrote it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And now, you lived with Moshe. And then where did you go from there, after living with your brother for a while?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, me, and my brother, and his wife, we went to Poland, the lodge.

speaker: interviewer
text: And from lodge, where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: From lodge, we went to Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wait a minute, hold the horses. [LAUGHS] I got-- no, I got married later. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We went to Gleiwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And why did you go there? What was--

speaker: interviewee
text: We went through Czechoslovakia. And we supposed to go father, and then they turn us back. That's why we wound up in Gleiwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: So approximately when were you in Gleiwitz? Was that still '45?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, end of-- '45, yeah, because the war finished. And when I was at my brother's, the war finished, in May, in '45.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now--

speaker: interviewee
text: So that was then, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And is Gleiwitz where you met your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and what is your husband's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wolf.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- Wolf Patoka.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, can you tell me about meeting him? What do you remember about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not much. You see, at that time, it wasn't like courting, a girl and a boy going together, out or something. At that time, it was like a [NON-ENGLISH]. They talk about it, and then that's how you get together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your brother know him, or did the families know each other?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I think so. Yeah. I knew his cousin real good. He was there too.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Gleiwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you met, and when did you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right there in Gleiwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was that, the approximate--

speaker: interviewee
text: Who knows.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the month you got--

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] about a date.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you remember what month?

speaker: interviewee
text: October, I think.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then, where did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no, it couldn't be, because we went to Germany. It's before that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know for sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you two go after getting married? You stayed in Gleiwitz, and then went--

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we went to Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: You didn't go on honeymoons that time. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Right, no. I know. I meant where did you go next to live.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Germany, in Pocking.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's a DP camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: DP camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what city is that near?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pocking is between Munchen and-- it's near Munchen, near Munchen.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and who went with you there, besides you and Wolf? Was your -- did Moshe go too?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, Moshe went to-- left for Austria, separate, separate. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. How long were you in--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, his cousin was there, I think-- his cousin, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you in Pocking?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. It was two, three years later, we went to Heidenheim.

speaker: interviewer
text: Also in Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: Also in Germany. It's near Heidelberg and Ohm. Then we went from Heidenheim, we went to Feldafing. And from Feldafing, we went to the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and when you decided to come to the United States-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] was there any relatives that you had in the United States? Was anybody here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I had a cousin.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so you-- you came and you came--

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't come to the cousin. There's-- his cousin was here in Baltimore. That's why we came here.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And when did you come to Baltimore? Do you recall?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Baltimore?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. Do you recall the-- the month and year of arriving?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was the 2nd of May. I know that, in 1951.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do? What did your husband do to earn a living, coming here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, right at first, we didn't do nothing. After we'd been here several months, his cousin loaned him some money. We opened up store on [INAUDIBLE] Street.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of store?

speaker: interviewee
text: A grocery store.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you work with him in the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: -- in the business? What was it called?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] Service.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, now, do you and Wolf have children?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had children the first year in America, not there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who are-- could you tell me your children's names and their ages?

speaker: interviewee
text: My oldest son, named Joseph, he born in 1955. And my next son, Israel, he's born-- he was born in 1957, in September. And my daughter, 1960.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Barbara.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you discuss with your children, as they were growing up, what you had gone through during the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Most of the time, my husband always talks about it a very little, a little bit.

speaker: interviewer
text: What effect do you think your experience during the Holocaust has had on the way you've raised your children?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know what to say. What effects-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know. I don't know what to say.

speaker: interviewer
text: Has your experience during war time affected your religious beliefs or observance?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe a little. I lost my belief. I don't have that belief that I had before. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Just some of it, but not as I used to be. I used to fall asleep and scream [INAUDIBLE]. Now I don't do it-- when I was a little child.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you give your children a Jewish education?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I gave them a Jewish education because I want them to know what they want themselves, not tell them. So they had a Jewish education. But they still don't go to the synagogue now. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: As we approach the end of this century--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's more than 52 years since the war is over.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there a final message you'd like to give to your family and the future generations who will watch this tape?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know what message I would give. I'd [INAUDIBLE] never forget what happened. Never forget what's been. Remember who we are. That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: And is there anything you'd like to add to your tape, to your story?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't know what to add. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: That's just it. Whatever they think is right, then do. Well, I want to thank you very much for spending the time and sharing your story with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's it?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Patoka, can you tell me who's in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, in the top row, on the right side is my sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's Chaya.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's Chaya. And then my two cousins, one name is Ita, and the other one is Penia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is Ita the one in the middle?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the middle is Ita. And then on the end is Penia. And on the bottom row is my cousin, right under my sister, Khasia. And the rest of them, I know them. But it's not my cousins.

speaker: interviewer
text: And about how old is your sister in this picture, would you say?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] it's before the war. I guess I don't know-- around 20, maybe. Maybe around 20 or 22, I'm not sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And who is in this photograph, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, that's my brother, Moshe, his wife on the right side, and his two children on the top.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Moshe went to live in Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And is he living now?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he died in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And who is this, that we're looking at?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my brother and his wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother Avrum. And on the left side is my brother and his-- Avrum-- and his wife Sonia, and the two children at the top, [INAUDIBLE] on the left.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where is-- Avrum moved to Baltimore?

speaker: interviewee
text: He lived in Baltimore. Avrum lived in Baltimore.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he's not living now?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he's not living now. His wife's not living. His son is not living. Only the daughter's living.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: This photograph is me and my brother Avrum.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who is in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: In this picture is my brother Nachman, his wife, and his son.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Nachman moved, also, to Israel, following--

speaker: interviewee
text: He was living in Israel. He died in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is my son Joseph and his wife Judy.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who are these children?

speaker: interviewee
text: These children are now my grandchildren, Erica and Michael, Joseph's children.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who is in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is me, my husband, and my two children, Barbara and Todd.

speaker: interviewer
text: They're on the left?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the left. And on the right side is Izzy and Denise.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this picture, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture's me, my husband-- wait a minute. That's me and Izzy, my son, and my husband, from the left. From the right, Barbara, my daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: At your 50th anniversary party.

speaker: interviewee
text: At my 50th anniversary. This is my husband Wolf, and my children, Izzy and his wife Denise.

speaker: interviewee
text: The story of Wolf and Rose Patoka, my father and my mother, is one of courage and love-- the courage they had to endure and to maintain. They had lost their families. They didn't even know that they had lost their own childhoods. Despite all of this that you've heard, they've grown. They raised a strong family. They live to enjoy every success of their children as if it were their own. I guess that's love, when someone else's joy feels better to you than your own joy. Well, maybe it is theirs, because their single goal in life is to provide for their children every opportunity and more that they didn't have. And in that regard, they were absolutely successful. I think I can speak for my sister Barbara and her husband Todd, Joe and Judy, and my wife Denise when I say your accomplishments are remarkable. There are no two people that I have greater respect for. We love you always.
